skill.name: mathjax-rendering
skill.description: Render mathematical formulas in Obsidian using LaTeX and MathJax syntax for equations, matrices, calculus, symbols, and notation.
skill.summary: Quick MathJax syntax guidance for writing mathematical notation in Obsidian notes.
skill.author: BitYoungjae

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported 388 command, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance patterns, but review found Markdown examples and LaTeX syntax only. The backticks are Markdown formatting, the weak crypto hits are ordinary documentation words, and the reconnaissance hit is probability notation. No evidence found for prompt injection, network access, secret access, or executable code.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Matched as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The reported external command findings are Markdown code fences and inline LaTeX examples. They document MathJax syntax and do not execute Ruby, shell commands, or user-controlled input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files are Markdown documentation with fenced examples and inline command names. No executable Ruby, shell invocation, or command runner is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Crypto Matched Ordinary Documentation Text
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections match words such as description, precedes, and spade in MathJax reference tables. No cryptographic algorithm, hashing API, or encryption code is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are YAML or symbol table text, not crypto implementation. The apparent matches are substring collisions with non-security documentation terms.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Probability Notation Matched as System Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance finding points to Bayes probability notation in a MathJax example. It does not query host identity, system state, files, or environment data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is inside a LaTeX probability example and contains only mathematical notation. There is no command, API call, or system information access nearby.

content.value_statement: Writing math in notes is slow when syntax is hard to remember. This skill provides focused MathJax patterns and references for Obsidian.
actual_capabilities: Explains inline and block MathJax delimiters for Obsidian notes.
Provides examples for fractions, roots, superscripts, and subscripts.
Documents common Greek letters, operators, relations, and symbols.
Shows matrix, aligned equation, cases, and delimiter patterns.
Covers calculus notation including integrals, sums, products, and limits.
Includes quick reference tables for frequent MathJax commands.
limitations: It is documentation only and does not render or validate equations directly.
It focuses on Obsidian MathJax syntax, not every LaTeX package feature.
It does not test compatibility across different Obsidian themes or plugins.
It does not solve mathematical problems or verify equation correctness.

use_cases.title: Create Lecture Notes
use_cases.description: Format calculus, algebra, and probability notes with readable inline and block equations.

use_cases.title: Document Technical Derivations
use_cases.description: Write aligned equations, matrices, and symbol-heavy derivations in Obsidian research notes.

use_cases.title: Prepare Teaching Materials
use_cases.description: Build consistent examples for formulas, definitions, and step-by-step mathematical explanations.

prompt_templates.title: Format a Simple Equation
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me the Obsidian MathJax syntax for writing a simple inline equation and a centered block equation.

prompt_templates.title: Convert Notes to MathJax
prompt_templates.scenario: Turn plain text math into Obsidian notation
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert my plain text formula into Obsidian MathJax and explain which parts use fractions, roots, or superscripts.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Matrix Example
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an Obsidian MathJax example for a matrix, its determinant, and an identity matrix with clear formatting.

prompt_templates.title: Structure a Derivation
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me write a multi-line derivation in Obsidian using aligned equations, cases, delimiters, and explanatory text.

output_examples.input: Format a physics note that includes the energy equation inside a sentence.
output_examples.output: The response places the equation inline so it stays readable within the paragraph and explains when inline math is appropriate.

output_examples.input: Create a clean linear algebra note with a two by two matrix and determinant.
output_examples.output: The response uses a displayed matrix, a determinant form, and short labels that match Obsidian MathJax conventions.

output_examples.input: Turn a piecewise definition into Obsidian math notation.
output_examples.output: The response uses a displayed cases layout and keeps condition text readable inside the mathematical expression.

best_practices: Use inline math for short expressions and block math for formulas that need visual focus.
Add braces around multi-character superscripts and subscripts to avoid rendering mistakes.
Use aligned equations for derivations so each transformation is easy to compare.
anti_patterns: Do not use block equations for every symbol in a paragraph.
Do not omit braces when an exponent or subscript contains more than one character.
Do not assume every LaTeX package command is supported by Obsidian MathJax.

faq.question: Does this skill render equations itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides syntax guidance for MathJax rendering inside Obsidian.

faq.question: Can it help with matrices?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes matrix environments, determinants, identity matrices, and ellipsis patterns.

faq.question: Does it cover Greek letters and symbols?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes common Greek letters, operators, relations, arrows, sets, and logic symbols.

faq.question: Is this useful outside Obsidian?
faq.answer: Some MathJax syntax is portable, but the guidance is written for Obsidian notes.

faq.question: Will it verify my mathematics?
faq.answer: No. It helps format notation but does not prove formulas or check mathematical correctness.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
